title: Origins in New Orleans
text: Jazz emerged in the late 19th and early 20th centuries in New Orleans, blending African rhythms, blues, ragtime, and European harmony. Early ensembles featured cornet, clarinet, trombone, tuba, and drums, with collective improvisation as the defining feature.

title: Ragtime as a precursor
text: Ragtime, popularized by Scott Joplin, predated jazz and brought syncopation to the mainstream. Though composed rather than improvised, its rhythmic feel directly influenced early jazz pianists and band leaders in the 1900s and 1910s.

title: Louis Armstrong and the soloist
text: Louis Armstrong shifted jazz from collective improvisation to the showcase of the virtuoso soloist. His Hot Five and Hot Seven recordings in the 1920s established the trumpet solo as the centerpiece and codified swing phrasing.

title: The Swing Era and big bands
text: From the mid-1930s through the mid-1940s, swing dominated American popular music. Big bands led by Duke Ellington, Count Basie, Benny Goodman, and Glenn Miller filled ballrooms; arrangements emphasized riffs, call-and-response, and dancers.

title: Duke Ellington as composer
text: Duke Ellington led his orchestra for nearly fifty years and wrote thousands of pieces tailored to specific players in his band. He treated the orchestra as his instrument and blurred the line between popular song and extended composition.

title: Bebop revolution
text: In the mid-1940s, bebop emerged in small clubs in Harlem. Charlie Parker and Dizzy Gillespie led a generation that favored complex harmony, breakneck tempos, and small combos over the dance-oriented big bands of swing.

title: Charlie Parker's vocabulary
text: Charlie Parker, known as Bird, built a melodic vocabulary based on chord substitutions, chromatic enclosures, and rhythmic displacement. His solos became required study for any bebop musician and remain a cornerstone of jazz pedagogy.

title: Cool jazz
text: Cool jazz arose in the late 1940s and early 1950s as a softer, more restrained reaction to bebop's intensity. Miles Davis's Birth of the Cool sessions, Gerry Mulligan, and Lennie Tristano typified the lighter timbre and arranged textures.

title: Hard bop
text: Hard bop developed in the mid-1950s as an East Coast response to cool jazz, reasserting blues, gospel, and R&B feeling. Art Blakey's Jazz Messengers, Horace Silver, and Clifford Brown were central figures of the style.

title: Modal jazz
text: Modal jazz, pioneered by Miles Davis and George Russell in the late 1950s, replaced rapid chord changes with extended scales or modes. Kind of Blue (1959) is the most famous modal album, with John Coltrane and Bill Evans on the date.

title: John Coltrane's sheets of sound
text: John Coltrane's playing evolved from bebop fluency to dense chordal explorations he called sheets of sound, then to modal and finally free expression. A Love Supreme (1965) is a spiritual landmark of post-bop saxophone.

title: Free jazz
text: Free jazz, associated with Ornette Coleman, Cecil Taylor, and late Coltrane, dispensed with fixed chord changes, meter, and conventional roles. It treated improvisation as collective exploration of sound and was often politically charged in the 1960s.

title: Bill Evans and trio interplay
text: Pianist Bill Evans reshaped the jazz trio with bassist Scott LaFaro and drummer Paul Motian. Rather than soloist-plus-accompaniment, the three players carried on continuous melodic conversation, especially on Sunday at the Village Vanguard (1961).

title: Fusion and Bitches Brew
text: Jazz fusion emerged at the end of the 1960s as players incorporated rock rhythms, electric instruments, and studio production. Miles Davis's Bitches Brew (1970) and groups like Weather Report and Return to Forever defined the style.

title: Smooth jazz
text: Smooth jazz crystallized in the 1980s as a radio-friendly outgrowth of fusion, with polished production, R&B grooves, and lyrical solos. Kenny G, George Benson, and Grover Washington Jr. became commercial mainstays.

title: Latin jazz
text: Latin jazz fuses jazz improvisation with Afro-Cuban and Brazilian rhythms. Dizzy Gillespie's collaborations with Chano Pozo in the late 1940s helped launch Afro-Cuban jazz; bossa nova brought a Brazilian strain in the early 1960s.

title: Bossa nova
text: Bossa nova emerged in Brazil in the late 1950s, combining samba rhythm with cool jazz harmony. Antonio Carlos Jobim, Joao Gilberto, and Stan Getz brought the style to American audiences with the 1964 album Getz/Gilberto.

title: ECM and European jazz
text: The German label ECM, founded by Manfred Eicher in 1969, cultivated a spacious, often introspective European jazz aesthetic. Keith Jarrett, Jan Garbarek, and Pat Metheny are among its signature artists.

title: Keith Jarrett solo concerts
text: Keith Jarrett's solo piano concerts, especially The Koln Concert (1975), invented an idiom of long-form spontaneous composition. He drew on jazz, classical, gospel, and folk in real time before live audiences.

title: Wynton Marsalis and neoclassicism
text: From the early 1980s, Wynton Marsalis championed a return to acoustic, swing-based, blues-rooted jazz and helped found Jazz at Lincoln Center. The neoclassical movement reasserted the tradition against fusion and the avant-garde.

title: M-Base and Steve Coleman
text: M-Base, led by saxophonist Steve Coleman in the late 1980s and 1990s, used odd meters, layered grooves, and structured improvisation to chart a path outside both neoclassicism and free jazz. Cassandra Wilson and Greg Osby were associated.

title: Contemporary jazz piano
text: Players like Brad Mehldau, Vijay Iyer, and Robert Glasper have reshaped jazz piano in the 2000s, drawing on pop songwriting, hip-hop production, and Indian rhythmic concepts while extending the trio format Bill Evans codified.

title: Jazz and hip-hop
text: From the early 1990s onward jazz and hip-hop have cross-pollinated, from A Tribe Called Quest sampling jazz records to Robert Glasper, Kamasi Washington, and Kendrick Lamar's To Pimp a Butterfly (2015) blending live jazz with rap.

title: Kamasi Washington and LA jazz
text: Saxophonist Kamasi Washington's The Epic (2015) brought a maximalist, spiritually charged jazz back into wide circulation and helped center a Los Angeles scene around the West Coast Get Down collective.

title: The Great American Songbook
text: Jazz musicians have long drawn repertoire from the Great American Songbook, the body of early 20th-century show tunes by Gershwin, Porter, Berlin, Kern, and Rodgers. Many became jazz standards reinterpreted across generations.

title: Jazz standards and the real book
text: A jazz standard is a tune that has entered the shared repertoire and is expected to be playable on the bandstand. The Real Book, an originally bootlegged lead-sheet compilation, codified the working canon for student musicians from the 1970s.

title: Improvisation and chord changes
text: Most jazz improvisation is built over a recurring harmonic form, the chord changes. Soloists develop melodic ideas that imply or outline the underlying chords, using scales, arpeggios, and learned vocabulary built up by years of practice.

title: Jam sessions and the bandstand
text: Jam sessions, informal after-hours gatherings where musicians sit in on standards, have always been the proving ground of jazz. They are where vocabulary is tested, reputations are made, and the unwritten rules of the bandstand are taught.

title: Women in jazz
text: Vocalists Ella Fitzgerald, Billie Holiday, Sarah Vaughan, and Betty Carter shaped the music; instrumentalists like pianist Mary Lou Williams, composer-arranger Carla Bley, and bandleader Maria Schneider have been central though long underrecognized.

title: Mary Lou Williams across eras
text: Pianist and composer Mary Lou Williams is unusual in having been a working professional across swing, bebop, and the avant-garde. She mentored Thelonious Monk and Dizzy Gillespie and continued composing sacred jazz into the 1970s.
